§ The 12 Cranial Nerves — Detailed Cards
I
Olfactory Nerve
Sensory (Special Visceral Afferent)
▼

Function

Smell (olfaction) — special sense of detecting odorous substances in the environment.

Key Anatomy

Olfactory receptor neurons in the olfactory epithelium (roof of nasal cavity, superior concha, nasal septum) → ~20 olfactory nerve bundles → pass through cribriform plate of ethmoid → synapse on mitral cells in the olfactory bulb → olfactory tract → piriform cortex (temporal lobe).

CN I is the only cranial nerve to enter the cerebrum directly. The olfactory bulbs/tracts are anterior extensions of the forebrain. Cell bodies: olfactory receptor neurons (bipolar) in the olfactory epithelium.

Cranial exit: Foramina in cribriform plate of ethmoid bone.

Clinical Correlation

Anosmia (Loss of Smell)

Associated with URIs, sinus disease, head trauma. Fracture of cribriform plate can tear olfactory nerve fibers → unilateral anosmia + CSF rhinorrhea. Frontal lobe tumors/meningiomas in the anterior cranial fossa can compress the olfactory bulb/tract.

Olfactory Hallucinations

Lesions irritating the lateral olfactory area (uncus) → "uncinate fits" = temporal lobe epilepsy with imaginary disagreeable odors and involuntary lip/tongue movements.

Testing: Blindfold patient, occlude one naris, present common odors (coffee). Test each side separately — anosmia is often unilateral.

II
Optic Nerve
Sensory (Special Somatic Afferent)
▼

Function

Vision — special sense from the retina.

Key Anatomy

Actually a CNS fiber tract (extension of diencephalon), not a true peripheral nerve. Formed by axons of retinal ganglion cells (3rd-order neurons). Surrounded by meninges + CSF → susceptible to demyelinating diseases like MS.

Retinal ganglion cells → optic nerve → exits orbit via optic canal → optic chiasm (nasal fibers decussate) → optic tract → lateral geniculate body (thalamus) → optic radiations → visual cortex (occipital lobe).

Cranial exit: Optic canal.

Clinical Correlation

Visual Field Defects

Optic nerve lesion → ipsilateral monocular blindness. Optic chiasm lesion (pituitary tumor) → bitemporal hemianopsia. Optic tract lesion → contralateral homonymous hemianopsia (most common visual field loss, seen in strokes).

Demyelinating Disease

Because CN II is myelinated by oligodendrocytes (CNS), it is vulnerable to MS — unlike other cranial nerves which use Schwann cells. Optic neuritis causes diminished visual acuity.

III
Oculomotor Nerve
Motor (Somatic + Parasympathetic)
▼

Function

Somatic motor: Superior rectus, inferior rectus, medial rectus, inferior oblique, levator palpebrae superioris (raises eyelid).

Parasympathetic (via ciliary ganglion): Sphincter pupillae (pupil constriction) + ciliary muscle (accommodation for near vision).

Key Anatomy

Nuclei: Oculomotor nucleus (somatic motor) + Edinger-Westphal nucleus (parasympathetic) — both in midbrain. Emerges from midbrain → lateral wall of cavernous sinus → enters orbit via superior orbital fissure → divides into superior division (SR, LPS) and inferior division (IR, MR, IO + parasympathetics to ciliary ganglion).

Clinical Correlation

CN III Palsy — "Down and Out" Pupil

Eye is abducted and depressed (only lateral rectus [CN VI] and superior oblique [CN IV] work). Pupil dilated (loss of parasympathetics). Ptosis (levator palpebrae paralyzed). First sign of CN III compression (e.g., uncal herniation) = ipsilateral sluggish pupillary response — autonomic fibers are superficial and affected first.

IV
Trochlear Nerve
Motor (Somatic)
▼

Function

Motor to superior oblique — depresses, abducts, and medially rotates (intorts) the eye. Assists looking down when the eye is adducted.

Key Anatomy

Smallest cranial nerve. Only CN to emerge from the posterior (dorsal) surface of the brainstem. Has the longest intracranial (subarachnoid) course. Nucleus in midbrain (caudal to CN III nucleus). Pierces dura at tentorium cerebelli → lateral wall of cavernous sinus → superior orbital fissure → superior oblique.

Clinical Correlation

Trochlear Nerve Palsy

Diplopia when looking down (especially going downstairs or reading). Long intracranial course makes it vulnerable to head trauma. Patient compensates by tilting head anteriorly and laterally toward the normal side. Unopposed extorsion by inferior oblique.

V
Trigeminal Nerve
Mixed — Sensory + Motor
▼

Function

Sensory: Principal somatic sensory nerve of the head — face, teeth, mouth, nasal cavity, paranasal sinuses, dura mater. Also distributes postsynaptic parasympathetics from all 4 cranial parasympathetic ganglia.

Motor (via V3 only): Muscles of mastication (masseter, temporalis, medial & lateral pterygoids), mylohyoid, anterior belly of digastric, tensor veli palatini, tensor tympani — all 1st pharyngeal arch derivatives.

Key Anatomy

Largest cranial nerve. Four nuclei: 1 motor + 3 sensory (mesencephalic, principal sensory, spinal). Emerges from lateral pons → large sensory root + small motor root → trigeminal ganglion (in trigeminal cave, lateral to cavernous sinus) → 3 divisions:
• V1 (Ophthalmic) — sensory only → superior orbital fissure
• V2 (Maxillary) — sensory only → foramen rotundum
• V3 (Mandibular) — sensory + motor → foramen ovale

Motor root bypasses trigeminal ganglion and merges with V3 immediately distal to it.

Cutaneous Sensory Distribution — Head & Neck (CN V)
Click image to zoom
Scalp Sensory Innervation — Trigeminal Divisions with Cervical Contributions
Click image to zoom

Clinical Correlation

Trigeminal Nerve Injury

Paralysis of muscles of mastication with jaw deviation toward the lesion side. Loss of pain/touch/thermal sensation in face. Loss of corneal reflex and sneeze reflex.

Trigeminal Neuralgia (Tic Douloureux)

Excruciating, episodic pain in V2/V3 distribution. Principal disease of the sensory root.

Dental Anesthesia

CN V is the sensory nerve of teeth and oral mucosa. Inferior alveolar nerve (V3) is the most frequently anesthetized nerve in the body. Maxillary teeth use infiltration technique because superior alveolar nerves (V2 branches) are not directly accessible.

VI
Abducent Nerve
Motor (Somatic)
▼

Function

Motor to lateral rectus — abducts the eye (turns it laterally).

Key Anatomy

Nucleus in pons. Emerges between pons and medulla → longest intradural course of any CN. Bends sharply over crest of petrous temporal bone → runs through the cavernous sinus (surrounded by venous blood, like the ICA) → superior orbital fissure → penetrates medial surface of lateral rectus.

Clinical Correlation

CN VI Palsy

Lateral rectus paralysis → eye is adducted (medially deviated) by unopposed medial rectus. Cannot abduct past midline. Diplopia in all gazes except toward opposite side. Vulnerable to raised ICP (long intradural course), cavernous sinus pathology, atherosclerotic ICA compression, and septic cavernous sinus thrombosis.

VII
Facial Nerve
Mixed — Motor + Sensory + Parasympathetic
▼

Function

Somatic (branchial) motor: Muscles of facial expression, stapedius, stylohyoid, posterior belly of digastric (2nd pharyngeal arch derivatives).

Parasympathetic: Pterygopalatine ganglion (lacrimal, nasal, palatine glands via greater petrosal nerve) + submandibular ganglion (sublingual & submandibular salivary glands via chorda tympani).

Special sensory: Taste from anterior 2/3 of tongue + soft palate (via chorda tympani → lingual nerve of V3).

Somatic sensory: Small area of skin on both aspects of the auricle.

Key Anatomy

Emerges at pontomedullary junction as 2 divisions: primary root (motor) + intermediate nerve (taste, parasympathetic, sensory). Enters internal acoustic meatus → facial canal in temporal bone (longest intra-osseous course of any CN) → geniculate ganglion (sensory ganglion) → branches off greater petrosal nerve, nerve to stapedius, chorda tympani → exits via stylomastoid foramen → enters parotid gland → 5 terminal motor branches: Temporal, Zygomatic, Buccal, Marginal mandibular, Cervical.

Clinical Correlation

Bell Palsy (LMN Lesion)

Most commonly paralyzed motor cranial nerve. Unilateral facial paralysis affecting both upper and lower face ipsilaterally. Eye cannot close, forehead cannot wrinkle, mouth droops. Often caused by viral neuritis compressing the nerve in the facial canal near stylomastoid foramen.

UMN vs LMN Lesion — Key Distinction

Central (UMN) lesion (stroke): Contralateral lower face only paralyzed — forehead SPARED because frontalis receives bilateral UMN innervation. Peripheral (LMN) lesion: Ipsilateral upper + lower face affected — forehead wrinkles gone.

Lesion Localization by Symptoms

Near geniculate ganglion = motor + taste + autonomic loss. Between ganglion and chorda tympani = same minus lacrimal function. Distal to stylomastoid foramen = motor only (e.g., parotid surgery).

VIII
Vestibulocochlear Nerve
Sensory (Special Somatic Afferent)
▼

Function

Hearing (cochlear division) + equilibrium, balance, and motion (vestibular division).

Key Anatomy

Emerges at pontomedullary junction → enters internal acoustic meatus (with CN VII + labyrinthine artery) → splits into:
• Vestibular nerve: Cell bodies in vestibular ganglion → maculae of utricle/saccule (linear acceleration, gravity) + cristae of semicircular duct ampullae (rotational acceleration).
• Cochlear nerve: Cell bodies in spiral ganglion → spiral organ (organ of Corti) for hearing.

Nuclei: Vestibular nuclei at pons-medulla junction (floor of 4th ventricle); cochlear nuclei in medulla.

Clinical Correlation

Acoustic Neuroma (Vestibular Schwannoma)

Benign tumor of Schwann cells on the vestibular nerve in the internal acoustic meatus. Progressive unilateral hearing loss (early symptom), tinnitus, dysequilibrium in ~70% of patients.

Deafness Types

Conductive: External/middle ear problem (otitis media). Sensorineural: Inner ear hair cell damage or cochlear pathway disease.

IX
Glossopharyngeal Nerve
Mixed — Motor + Sensory + Parasympathetic
▼

Function

Somatic (branchial) motor: Stylopharyngeus only (3rd pharyngeal arch).

Parasympathetic: Presynaptic fibers via tympanic nerve → lesser petrosal nerve → otic ganglion → parotid gland (secretomotor) via auriculotemporal nerve (V3).

Special sensory: Taste from posterior 1/3 of tongue.

Somatic sensory: Posterior 1/3 of tongue, oropharynx, fauces, soft palate, tympanic cavity, pharyngotympanic tube.

Visceral sensory: Carotid body (chemoreceptor — O₂/CO₂) + carotid sinus (baroreceptor — blood pressure).

Key Anatomy

Emerges from lateral medulla → exits via jugular foramen (with CN X, XI). Superior & inferior ganglia at jugular foramen. Follows stylopharyngeus between superior & middle pharyngeal constrictors to reach oropharynx and tongue. Afferent limb of gag reflex (CN X = efferent limb).

Clinical Correlation

Jugular Foramen (Vernet) Syndrome

Tumors near jugular foramen affect CN IX, X, XI together → dysphagia, dysarthria, "curtain sign" (palate/pharyngeal wall deviate to contralateral side on gag reflex), loss of taste posterior tongue, shoulder weakness.

Gag Reflex

Absent in ~37% of normal individuals. CN IX = afferent, CN X = efferent. Touch posterior pharyngeal wall to test.

X
Vagus Nerve
Mixed — Motor + Sensory + Parasympathetic
▼

Function

Somatic (branchial) motor: Pharyngeal constrictors, intrinsic laryngeal muscles (phonation), palate muscles (except tensor veli palatini), palatoglossus, striated muscle of upper 2/3 esophagus.

Parasympathetic: Smooth muscle & glands of tracheobronchial tree, heart (cardiac plexus), esophagus, entire GI tract to the left colic (splenic) flexure.

Visceral sensory: Thoracic & abdominal organs; pharynx, larynx (afferent limb of cough reflex).

Special sensory: Taste from epiglottis + root of tongue.

Somatic sensory: Posterior auricle, tragus, external acoustic meatus.

Key Anatomy

Longest course & widest distribution of any CN ("vagus" = wandering). Emerges from lateral medulla → exits via jugular foramen. Superior ganglion (in foramen, somatic sensory) + inferior/nodose ganglion (below foramen, visceral/taste sensory). Descends in carotid sheath. Key branches: pharyngeal, superior laryngeal (internal = sensory, external = motor to cricothyroid), recurrent laryngeal nerves (all other intrinsic laryngeal muscles). Asymmetric thoracic course → esophageal plexus → anterior + posterior vagal trunks → abdomen.

Clinical Correlation

Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve Injury

Unilateral: Dysphonia (hoarseness) from vocal fold paralysis. Bilateral: Aphonia + inspiratory stridor (emergency). Left recurrent laryngeal nerve lesions more common due to longer course (loops under aortic arch). Common causes: thyroid surgery, aortic aneurysm, lung cancer, esophageal surgery.

Vagus Nerve Lesion

Soft palate sags, uvula deviates toward normal side. Dysphagia. Efferent limb of gag reflex (CN IX = afferent). The "cranial root of CN XI" is actually part of CN X.

XI
Spinal Accessory Nerve
Motor (Somatic)
▼

Function

Motor to sternocleidomastoid (SCM) and trapezius.

Key Anatomy

Arises from spinal cord (C1–C5/C6 anterior horn motor neurons), NOT the brainstem. Rootlets ascend through foramen magnum → briefly joins CN X → exits via jugular foramen. Descends along ICA → penetrates & innervates SCM → crosses posterior triangle (superficially!) → deep to trapezius. Picks up C2–C4 sensory fibers (pain, proprioception) from cervical plexus in the lateral cervical region.

Clinical Correlation

CN XI Injury — Iatrogenic Risk

Most common cause is iatrogenic — lymph node biopsy, IJV cannulation, carotid endarterectomy (nerve is nearly subcutaneous in the posterior triangle). High lesion → SCM + trapezius paralysis (impaired head rotation toward affected side + inability to shrug shoulder + scapular winging). Low lesion → trapezius only (more common).

Testing: Ask patient to shrug shoulders against resistance (trapezius) and turn head against resistance (SCM — turns head to opposite side).

XII
Hypoglossal Nerve
Motor (Somatic)
▼

Function

Motor to all intrinsic and extrinsic tongue muscles EXCEPT palatoglossus (which is innervated by CN X — it's a palatine muscle).

Extrinsic muscles supplied: styloglossus, hyoglossus, genioglossus.

Key Anatomy

Arises as rootlets from medulla (between pyramids and olives) → exits via hypoglossal canal. After exiting, is joined by C1/C2 fibers from cervical plexus that "hitch a ride" (these form the superior root of ansa cervicalis and nerve to thyrohyoid — these are NOT true CN XII fibers). Passes medial to angle of mandible → enters tongue between mylohyoid and hyoglossus.

Clinical Correlation

CN XII Palsy — Tongue Deviation

On protrusion, tongue deviates toward the lesion side (paralyzed genioglossus cannot push that side forward). Over time → ipsilateral tongue atrophy (shrunken, wrinkled). Dysarthria. Most trauma is iatrogenic (carotid endarterectomy, intubation). Eagle syndrome — elongated styloid process compresses CN XII during head rotation.

Testing: "Stick out your tongue." Watch for deviation.

§ Quick Reference Table
All 12 Cranial Nerves — At a Glance
#NameTypeKey Function(s)Cranial ExitCNS Origin
IOlfactorySensorySmellCribriform plate foraminaForebrain (telencephalon)
IIOpticSensoryVisionOptic canalDiencephalon
IIIOculomotorMotor*Most eye movement + pupil constriction + accommodationSuperior orbital fissureMidbrain
IVTrochlearMotorSuperior oblique (eye down & in)Superior orbital fissureMidbrain (dorsal!)
VTrigeminalBothFace sensation (V1-V3) + muscles of mastication (V3)SOF / For. rotundum / For. ovalePons
VIAbducentMotorLateral rectus (eye abduction)Superior orbital fissurePons-medulla junction
VIIFacialBothFacial expression + taste ant. 2/3 tongue + lacrimation/salivationIAM → stylomastoid foramenPontomedullary junction
VIIIVestibulocochlearSensoryHearing + balanceInternal acoustic meatusPontomedullary junction
IXGlossopharyngealBothTaste post. 1/3 tongue + gag reflex afferent + parotid gland + stylopharyngeusJugular foramenMedulla
XVagusBothParasympathetic to thoracic/abdominal viscera + phonation + cough reflexJugular foramenMedulla
XISpinal AccessoryMotorSCM + trapeziusJugular foramenSpinal cord (C1–C5/6)
XIIHypoglossalMotorTongue muscles (except palatoglossus)Hypoglossal canalMedulla
* CN III also carries parasympathetic fibers (Edinger-Westphal nucleus). CN VII, IX, X also carry parasympathetic fibers. IAM = Internal Acoustic Meatus; SOF = Superior Orbital Fissure.
§ Clinical Summary — Lesions at a Glance
High-Yield Cranial Nerve Lesion Findings (Table 10.6)
CNCommon Lesion CauseKey Finding(s)
ICribriform plate fractureAnosmia; CSF rhinorrhea
IIOptic canal fracture; demyelinationVisual field defects; loss of pupillary constriction
IIIUncal herniation; cavernous sinus pathologyDilated pupil; ptosis; eye "down and out"
IVHead trauma (long course)Diplopia looking down; head tilt to compensate
VTrigeminal neuralgia; tumorsLoss of facial sensation; jaw deviation toward lesion; loss of corneal reflex
VIRaised ICP; cavernous sinus thrombosisMedial deviation of eye; diplopia on lateral gaze
VIIBell palsy; temporal bone fracture; parotid surgeryFacial paralysis (LMN = entire face; UMN = lower face only); loss of taste ant. 2/3 tongue
VIIIAcoustic neuromaProgressive unilateral hearing loss; tinnitus; vertigo
IXJugular foramen tumors; tonsillectomyLoss of gag reflex afferent; loss of taste post. 1/3 tongue
XRecurrent laryngeal nerve injury (thyroid surgery, aortic aneurysm)Hoarseness (unilateral); aphonia + stridor (bilateral); uvula deviates to normal side
XIIatrogenic (posterior triangle surgery)Shoulder droop; inability to shrug; impaired head rotation
XIIIatrogenic (carotid endarterectomy); Eagle syndromeTongue deviates toward lesion; ipsilateral atrophy; dysarthria
§ Cranial Parasympathetic Ganglia — High Yield
4 Parasympathetic Ganglia — All Associated with CN V Branches
GanglionParent Nerve (Presynaptic)CN V Branch (Distribution)Target
CiliaryCN III (Edinger-Westphal)V1 (nasociliary n.)Sphincter pupillae + ciliary muscle
PterygopalatineCN VII (greater petrosal n.)V2 (ganglionic branches)Lacrimal gland + nasal/palatine glands
OticCN IX (lesser petrosal n.)V3 (auriculotemporal n.)Parotid gland
SubmandibularCN VII (chorda tympani)V3 (lingual n.)Submandibular + sublingual glands
CN V conveys NO parasympathetic fibers from the CNS — but all 4 ganglia are associated with its branches, which distribute the postsynaptic fibers.
§ Key Mnemonics
Nerve Names (I–XII)
Oh, Oh, Oh, To Touch And Feel Very Good Velvet, Ah Heavenly
Sensory / Motor / Both
Some Say Money Matters But My Brother Says Big Brains Matter More
CN VII Terminal Branches
To Zanzibar By Motor Car = Temporal, Zygomatic, Buccal, Marginal mandibular, Cervical
Parasympathetic Ganglia Parent Nerves
CN III → Ciliary  |  CN VII → Pterygopalatine + Submandibular  |  CN IX → Otic

§ Self-Test MCQs
15 single-best-answer questions. The final five are clinical vignettes. Answer positions are deliberately mixed, and the options are kept similar in length so you can test recall rather than pattern-spotting.
Q1 Core concept
A patient has ptosis, an eye resting “down and out,” and a dilated pupil. Which nerve is most likely affected?
Answer: B — Oculomotor nerve
CN III supplies most extraocular muscles, levator palpebrae superioris, and parasympathetic fibers to the pupil. A lesion classically causes ptosis, a “down and out” eye, and mydriasis.
Q2 Reflex arc
Touching the right cornea produces no blink in either eye. Which lesion best explains this finding?
Answer: D — Right ophthalmic division
The afferent limb of the corneal reflex is CN V1 via the nasociliary branch. If the right cornea is touched and neither eye blinks, the sensory input from the right side has failed.
Q3 Extraocular muscles
Which extraocular muscle is innervated by the trochlear nerve?
Answer: A — Superior oblique
CN IV innervates only one muscle: superior oblique. It primarily intorts the eye and helps depress the adducted eye, which is why diplopia is often worse on stairs or when reading.
Q4 Parasympathetics
Parasympathetic fibers traveling with CN III synapse in which ganglion before constricting the pupil?
Answer: C — Ciliary ganglion
Preganglionic parasympathetic fibers from the Edinger–Westphal nucleus travel with CN III and synapse in the ciliary ganglion. Postganglionic fibers then reach sphincter pupillae and ciliary muscle through short ciliary nerves.
Q5 Divisions of V
Loss of sensation over the lower jaw together with weak mastication points to which division?
Answer: B — Mandibular division
V3 is the only trigeminal division with both sensory and motor components. It supplies sensation to the lower face and motor innervation to the muscles of mastication.
Q6 Taste + gag
Which nerve carries taste from the posterior one-third of the tongue and provides the afferent limb of the gag reflex?
Answer: A — Glossopharyngeal nerve
CN IX conveys taste and general sensation from the posterior third of the tongue. It is also the main afferent limb of the gag reflex, whereas CN X provides the major efferent response.
Q7 Motor testing
After posterior triangle surgery, a patient cannot shrug the right shoulder against resistance. Which nerve is most likely injured?
Answer: D — Spinal accessory nerve
CN XI supplies sternocleidomastoid and trapezius. Injury in the posterior triangle classically causes weak shoulder elevation and difficulty turning the head against resistance.
Q8 Tongue movement
On protrusion, the tongue deviates to the right and shows ipsilateral atrophy. Which structure is injured?
Answer: C — Right hypoglossal nerve
A lower motor neuron lesion of CN XII causes weakness, wasting, and fasciculations of the ipsilateral tongue. On protrusion, the tongue deviates toward the side of the lesion.
Q9 Parotid secretomotor
Postganglionic parasympathetic fibers from the otic ganglion reach the parotid gland through which nerve?
Answer: B — Auriculotemporal nerve
CN IX sends preganglionic fibers via the tympanic plexus and lesser petrosal nerve to the otic ganglion. Postganglionic fibers then hitchhike on the auriculotemporal nerve, a branch of V3, to the parotid gland.
Q10 Palate + larynx
Hoarseness, impaired palate elevation, and dysphagia most strongly suggest damage to which nerve?
Answer: A — Vagus nerve
CN X supplies muscles of the palate, pharynx, and larynx. Lesions can produce dysphagia, hoarseness, reduced palatal elevation, and deviation of the uvula away from the affected side.
Q11 Clinical vignette
A 24-year-old develops anosmia after striking the bridge of the nose in a bicycle crash. Fracture through which structure most likely disrupted the injured nerve fibers?
Answer: D — Cribriform plate
Olfactory nerve filaments pass from the nasal cavity through the cribriform plate to reach the olfactory bulb. Shearing at this site is a classic cause of post-traumatic anosmia.
Q12 Clinical vignette
A patient complains of vertical diplopia that is worst when reading or walking downstairs. Which nerve is most likely affected?
Answer: C — Trochlear nerve
Superior oblique helps depress the adducted eye. CN IV palsy therefore produces vertical diplopia that is especially troublesome on stairs or when reading, often with a compensatory head tilt.
Q13 Clinical vignette
After removal of a parotid tumor, a patient has inability to close the eye, loss of taste from the anterior tongue, and hyperacusis. Which nerve was injured?
Answer: B — Facial nerve
CN VII carries motor fibers to muscles of facial expression, taste fibers from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue, and nerve to stapedius. Combined facial weakness, taste loss, and hyperacusis point strongly to a facial nerve lesion.
Q14 Clinical vignette
Following thyroid surgery, a patient develops hoarseness and dysphagia. On saying “ah,” the uvula deviates to the left. Which nerve was most likely injured?
Answer: A — Right vagus nerve
A palatal weakness from right CN X dysfunction pulls the uvula toward the intact left side. Hoarseness and dysphagia fit the same localization because vagus also innervates laryngeal and pharyngeal muscles.
Q15 Clinical vignette
After tonsillectomy, a patient loses taste and general sensation from the posterior one-third of the tongue and has a reduced gag response. Which nerve was injured?
Answer: D — Glossopharyngeal nerve
CN IX supplies taste and general sensation to the posterior third of the tongue and is the main afferent limb of the gag reflex. It is vulnerable in tonsillar surgery because of its close relationship to the tonsillar bed.
Figure view
